title: Modern Two-Drawer Nightstand
summary: A plywood box on splayed 10° legs, two drawers on euro slides over an open shelf — a clean mid-century profile with no visible plywood edges.
description:
This nightstand is a plywood box — top, bottom, sides, and a center shelf — set on four legs splayed outward at 10°, which is what gives it the mid-century lean rather than reading as a plain rectangular cabinet on straight legs. The angle is small enough that it doesn't need a jig: cut it on a miter saw or with a circular saw and a guide, same as any other angled cut.

Drawer boxes are simple butt-jointed 1x4 frames with a 1/4" plywood bottom, riding on bottom-mount euro slides rather than traditional wood runners — cheap, self-closing, and forgiving of small dimensional errors in the drawer box itself, which matters because there's no adjustment built into a butt-jointed drawer the way there is with dovetails. Drawer fronts are separate pieces attached after the drawer boxes are built and hung, which is what lets you get consistent, even reveals around each drawer instead of trying to hit an exact opening size on the first try.

tools.note: Cuts the 10° angled legs cleanly and repeatably.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, half sheet (4x4)
materials.note: Rip into 15-3/4"-wide pieces for the box top, bottom, and shelf.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, half sheet
materials.note: Drawer bottoms and the cabinet back.

materials.name: 1x2 board, 8 ft
materials.note: The four angled legs and base rails.

materials.name: 1x4 board, 6 ft
materials.note: Drawer box sides, fronts, and backs.

materials.name: 1x6 board, 3 ft
materials.note: The two drawer faces — plan assumes a true 5-1/2" wide board.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends parallel to each other.
Both ends cut at 10° off square, ends NOT parallel to each other.

steps.title: Rip and cut the plywood carcass parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4"-wide strips and cross-cut the top, bottom, side, and shelf/divider pieces to length. Drill 3/4" pocket holes facing upward in the top edges of the front and back aprons now, while they're loose and easy to clamp — you'll use these to attach the box to the base later.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Pocket-screw and glue the top, bottom, side, and center shelf/divider together into a simple box, open on the drawer side. Attach the front and back aprons, then nail the plywood back panel on square — it's what keeps the whole cabinet from racking.

steps.title: Build the angled leg base
steps.body: Join the four 10°-angled legs with the side and front/back rails into a base frame. Because the legs splay outward, dry-fit the base and check it sits flat and stable on the floor before you commit any glue — an angled-leg base amplifies small cutting errors more than a square one would.

steps.title: Attach the box to the base
steps.body: Set the box on top of the leg base and drive screws up through the pocket holes in the aprons into the base rails.

steps.title: Build and hang the drawers
steps.body: Build two drawer boxes from the 1x4 sides and fronts/backs, glue and nail on the plywood bottoms square. Install the euro slides on both the drawer boxes and inside the cabinet per the hardware's instructions, and hang the drawers — check they roll smoothly before moving on.

steps.title: Attach drawer faces and pulls
steps.body: With the drawers hung, attach the drawer faces to the fronts of the drawer boxes with screws from inside, using spacers to get an even reveal around each face before driving anything permanent. Add pulls, sand every surface, and finish.

images.alt: Modern two-drawer nightstand with splayed legs and an open bottom shelf
